learning_objectives: Navigate Florida's comprehensive laser registration system
Understand Georgia's federal-deference approach to regulation
Compare state-specific requirements for entertainment laser shows
Identify special local requirements (Orlando Fire, etc.)

phases.title: Scenario Warm-Up: The Southeast Tour
phases.description: You're planning shows in Miami, Orlando, and Atlanta. What regulatory differences will you encounter?

interactive_element.prompt: Select each city to discover the regulatory landscape:

locations.name: Miami Beach Convention Center
jurisdictions: Florida DOH Bureau of Radiation Control
locations.special_notes: Registration required for all Class IIIB/IV lasers

locations.name: Orlando Arena
locations.special_notes: Orlando Fire Rescue has special requirements for laser shows with atmospheric effects

locations.name: Atlanta Music Venue
locations.special_notes: Georgia defers to federal regulations; no separate state registration

feedback.correct_understanding: Correct! Florida has comprehensive state registration, while Georgia follows federal guidelines with no additional state layer.
feedback.key_insight: Florida's registration system is one of the more comprehensive in the Southeast.

phases.title: Core Concept: Florida's Registration System
phases.description: Understanding Florida's comprehensive laser device registration program

content_sections.title: Statutory Authority
content_sections.content: Florida regulates lasers under Chapter 501.122, Florida Statutes, and Chapter 64E-4, Florida Administrative Code.
key_points: All laser devices (except exempted) must be registered
Applies to both users AND manufacturers
Registration is FREE (no fee)
One-time registration per device

content_sections.title: Device Classification Requirements
content_sections.content: Florida uses standard laser classification with specific registration triggers.
key_points: Class IV: ≥0.5 watts (medical surgery, laser shows, cutters, welders)
Class IIIB: >0.005W but <0.5W (small laser shows, surveying, range finders)
Exemptions apply to certain consumer products
Registration required within 30 days of acquisition

content_sections.title: Laser Safety Officer Requirements
content_sections.content: Florida requires designation of a qualified Laser Safety Officer.
key_points: LSO must be listed on registration form
Qualified by training and experience
Authority to establish radiation protection program
Responsibility for facility or mobile laser safety

content_sections.title: Out-of-State Equipment
content_sections.content: Special requirements apply to temporary laser use in Florida.
key_points: 20-day advance written notice required
Notify Bureau of Radiation Control before bringing device into state
Applies to entertainment lasers for laser light shows
Each location requires separate consideration

items.details: Class IIIB or IV requires registration
Must be qualified by training and experience
Send to FL Department of Health, Bureau of Radiation Control
If device is moved, altered, destroyed, or sold

phases.title: Core Concept: Georgia's Federal Deference Model
phases.description: Understanding Georgia's approach of following federal guidelines

content_sections.title: Georgia Regulatory Approach
content_sections.content: Georgia does not maintain separate state laser regulations. Instead, the state follows federal FDA guidelines.
key_points: No state laser registration required
No state-specific licensing program
Follows 21 CFR 1040 (FDA regulations)
Federal variance required for entertainment shows

content_sections.title: Required Documentation
content_sections.content: While Georgia doesn't require state registration, proper documentation is essential.
key_points: FDA Variance (required for entertainment)
Standard Operating Procedures (SOP)
Quality Control Safety Checklists
Health and Safety Report with Risk Assessment
Operation Log Sheets

content_sections.title: FAA Coordination for Outdoor Shows
content_sections.content: Georgia is in the FAA Eastern Service Area.
key_points: Submit FAA Form 7140 for outdoor shows
Email: 9-ATO-ESA-OSG-Lasers-Searchlights@faa.gov
Required for unterminated outdoor laser operations
Coordinate with local venues on safety requirements

documents.name: FDA Variance Application
documents.purpose: License to perform laser light shows

documents.name: Standard Operating Procedures
documents.purpose: Description of safe laser show procedures

documents.name: Health and Safety Report
documents.purpose: Risk assessment with show info, diagrams, training proof

phases.title: State Comparator: Florida vs Georgia
phases.description: Interactive comparison of the two states

categories.florida: Required - must be designated on registration

categories.note: Both may have local fire/safety coordination

categories.name: Timeline to Compliant Operation
categories.note: Florida takes longer due to state layer

Individual venues may have additional safety requirements

phases.title: Challenge: The Florida-Georgia Circuit
phases.description: Navigate complex interstate operations between Florida and Georgia

interactive_element.title: The Cross-Border Festival
interactive_element.scenario: You're operating a Class 4 laser show system for a festival circuit. Show #1 is in Orlando, FL on June 15th. Show #2 is in Atlanta, GA on June 22nd. Your equipment is currently registered in Texas. Today is May 20th.

questions.question: What's the critical deadline for Florida compliance?
questions.explanation: Florida requires 20-day advance notice for out-of-state equipment. May 20 + 20 days = May 26. The show is June 15, so May 26 is the last day to notify and still meet the 20-day requirement.

questions.question: For the Atlanta show, what additional documentation is needed beyond FDA variance?
questions.explanation: Georgia doesn't require state licensing. However, if it's an outdoor show, FAA Form 7140 would be required. The question mentions Atlanta (major city) - typically indoor venues don't need FAA, but outdoor would.

questions.question: The Orlando venue wants to use atmospheric fog effects. What additional coordination is required?
questions.explanation: Orlando/Orange County Fire Rescue has special requirements for laser shows with atmospheric effects like fog/haze. This is a local requirement beyond state registration.

quiz_questions.question: Which Florida agency regulates laser devices?
options: Florida Department of Health - Bureau of Radiation Control
quiz_questions.explanation: The Florida Department of Health Bureau of Radiation Control regulates laser devices in Florida.

quiz_questions.question: Florida charges a fee for laser device registration.
quiz_questions.explanation: Florida laser registration is FREE. There is no fee for registering laser devices with the state.

quiz_questions.question: How many days in advance must out-of-state laser operators notify Florida?
quiz_questions.explanation: Florida requires 20-day advance written notice before bringing out-of-state laser equipment into the state for temporary use.

quiz_questions.question: What is Georgia's approach to laser regulation?
options: Follows federal FDA guidelines with no separate state requirements
Requires state laser license for all Class 4 operations
quiz_questions.explanation: Georgia defers to federal FDA regulations and does not maintain separate state laser registration or licensing requirements.

quiz_questions.question: A laser show company with Texas-registered equipment plans shows in Orlando and Atlanta next month. What must they do?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Notify Florida 20 days in advance; Georgia requires no state registration but follow federal guidelines
quiz_questions.explanation: Florida requires 20-day notice for out-of-state equipment. Georgia has no state registration, so federal variance compliance is sufficient. Both require FDA variance.

key_takeaways: Florida requires FREE state registration for Class IIIB/IV lasers
Florida requires 20-day advance notice for out-of-state equipment
Georgia defers entirely to federal FDA regulations
Florida requires a designated Laser Safety Officer on registration
Orlando/Orange County Fire has special requirements for atmospheric effects
Registration is one-time in Florida but must be updated for location changes
Timeline: Florida = 3-4 weeks (with notice), Georgia = 2-3 weeks
